prompt:
Ticket from the platform-storage rotation. Our config service keeps its state in one process and writes each key change to disk individually — a crash between two related writes leaves half an update applied, and under load we fsync per key which is killing p99. I want a small transactional KV with a redo log and group commit. Module go-txnredo, package txnredo, this repo, stdlib only.

Open(path string, opts *Options) (*DB, error) opens or creates the redo log at exactly path (nil opts is fine). Options has one field: Sync func() error — the durability barrier. When it's non-nil the DB must call it exactly once per flushed batch, after that batch's bytes have been written to the file; nil means fsync the file instead. Tests inject counters and blockers through this hook, so the call discipline is part of the contract.

DB API: Begin() *Tx, Get(key string) ([]byte, bool), Keys() []string (sorted), Pending() int, Close() error. Tx API: Set(key string, value []byte) error, Delete(key string) error, Get(key string) ([]byte, bool), Commit() error, Abort() error. Transactions are the only write path. A Tx sees its own uncommitted writes layered over committed state (read-your-writes, including its own deletes); other transactions and DB.Get never see uncommitted data. No conflict detection — last committer wins, document that in a comment. Empty keys are rejected by Set. Values are copied both directions: callers mutating a passed-in or returned slice must never affect the store. After Commit or Abort a Tx is dead: every method returns ErrTxDone (errors.Is-able). After Close, commits fail with ErrClosed; Close is idempotent. A Commit with no ops succeeds without touching the log or calling Sync.

The log: one checksummed record per committed transaction, holding all its ops — the record is the atomicity unit. Format is yours, but recovery has to hold: Open replays valid records in order; at the first torn record (cut mid-bytes), corrupt record (checksum mismatch — assume any flipped byte is caught), or trailing garbage, it stops, applies nothing from that record even if most of its ops were readable, and truncates the file back to the end of the last valid record so the log is immediately writable again. A transaction is all-or-nothing across every key it touched.

Group commit is the point of the exercise: while one batch's flush is in flight, arriving commits must not each pay their own sync — they enqueue, Pending() reports how many are waiting, and when the in-flight flush returns the entire queue is written and made durable with exactly ONE more Sync call. Every commit returns only after the sync covering its record succeeds. Sequential lone commits are one sync each. If Sync fails, that batch must not be acknowledged anywhere: its bytes come back off the file (truncate to the pre-batch offset), every commit in the batch gets the error, and nothing is applied in memory — a later reopen must not resurrect it.

The DB must be safe for concurrent use. txnredo_test.go and recovery_test.go are the acceptance contract — don't touch them; go test -race -timeout 30s ./... must pass.
